items.title: Manifest-driven deploy with dry-run, backup-on-conflict and audit
items.problem: Deploy logic is implicit: link-dotfiles.sh links every root dotfile by convention, OS/host variance lives in .Darwin/.Linux suffixes and per-host .bashrc.<hostname> sprawl, and there is no way to preview or audit what a run will do.
items.you: Bespoke Makefile + link-dotfiles.sh with interactive diff/merge; filename-suffix OS branching.
items.them: A manifest declares source→target mappings with per-entry OS/host conditions; deploy supports --dry-run, backs up conflicting files, and an audit subcommand reports drift.
items.difference: Reviewability and safety: a declared plan you can diff, preview and audit vs a convention you have to re-read shell to understand. Replaces suffix/per-host sprawl with explicit conditions.
items.translation: Steal the design, not anyone's unreviewed engine: a manifest file + a small bash driver alongside link-dotfiles.sh, migrating a few files at a time.
items.pilot: Manifest covering only bin.Darwin/bin.Linux and one per-host file, run in --dry-run against current state; link-dotfiles.sh keeps handling the rest until parity.

items.title: Secrets hygiene: plaintext/permissions audit and systematic op run wrapping
items.problem: Secrets storage is solved (1Password CLI: op:// references + op run/op inject), but usage may be ad hoc and nothing checks for leaked plaintext or bad file permissions.
items.you: 1Password CLI with references-not-values; no audit; op run wrapping applied inconsistently.
items.them: Store-agnostic hygiene layer: an audit for plaintext secrets and permission problems, plus consistent wrapper entrypoints (e.g. a withsecrets one-off).
items.difference: Not a store switch (rejected: op works, alternatives are newer/riskier) — the remainder is hygiene tooling that catches leaks regardless of store.
items.translation: Audit folds into the manifest-deploy audit item if adopted; otherwise a standalone bin/ script plus documented op run conventions in the relevant rc files.
items.spike_question: Would a plaintext/permissions audit of $HOME dotfiles find anything today, and is op usage inconsistent enough to justify a withsecrets wrapper?

items.title: Real dotfiles test suite (per-file test_*.sh)
items.problem: CI only smoke-tests that make link-dotfiles completes on macOS; nothing asserts the resulting environment actually works.
items.them: Per-file test_*.sh scripts emitting RESULT run=N failed=N, aggregated by a tiny runner.
items.difference: Regressions in shell config become visible in CI instead of at next login. Low effort, real value.
items.translation: tests/ dir + make test target wired into ci.yml, bash-first like everything else.

items.title: Declarative macOS defaults (desired-state + drift) replacing imperative .macos
items.problem: .macos is a one-way imperative script: no record of desired state, no way to detect drift after OS updates or manual changes.
items.them: Desired-state table (e.g. TSV of domain/key/type/value) with an apply mode and a drift-report mode.
items.difference: Drift detection and reviewable state vs write-only script. Optional: current approach works.
items.translation: TSV + small bash applier behind the existing make macos-* targets; .macos retires incrementally.

items.title: mise instead of asdf
items.problem: Not a gap-fill — runtimes are already pinned via asdf/.tool-versions. This is replace-a-working-tool.
items.them: mise: shim-free PATH, per-directory [env], built-in task runner, extra install backends; reads .tool-versions natively.
items.difference: Ergonomics and speed, not capability. Incremental benefit unless paired with a broader rebuild.
items.translation: Brewfile swap; keep .tool-versions so the change is reversible.
items.spike_question: Does mise handle every runtime in .tool-versions unchanged, and does shim-free PATH measurably improve shell startup?

items.title: zsh migration (or ble.sh in-place) for interactive ergonomics
items.problem: Interactive bash lacks modern line-editor ergonomics (autosuggestions, syntax highlighting) that zsh ecosystems provide.
items.you: bash-first via Homebrew bash + chsh; zsh files present but secondary.
items.them: zsh as interactive shell with plugin ecosystem; alternatively ble.sh adds similar features to bash in place.
items.difference: Interactive UX only — scripts stay bash regardless, so blast radius is the interactive login path.
items.translation: Pilot via .config/zsh/.zshrc + ZDOTDIR in a subshell before any chsh; compare against a ble.sh trial in current bash.
items.pilot: ZDOTDIR subshell pilot for two weeks of real use; no chsh until it sticks.
